abstract: This systematic review examines the use of social media data in empirical social science research from 2010 to 2024, analyzing 59 journals across five disciplines (Communication, Economics, Political Science, Psychology, Sociology) plus interdisciplinary outlets. Using a human-in-the-loop LLM annotation pipeline, the authors estimate that 0.78% of papers in their corpus (3.15% in social science journals alone) use social media data for empirical research, with usage concentrated on Twitter/X and Facebook. The share of such papers tripled over the period but plateaued and declined in 2022-2024, coinciding with restrictions on platform data access, suggesting that the empirical study of social media may be on the cusp of significant transformation.
key_claims: Empirical social media research constitutes a small but non-trivial share of social science publishing (0.78% overall; 3.15% excluding interdisciplinary journals), with large cross-disciplinary variation (from <1% in Economics to ~13% in Communication).
Twitter/X (50.86%) and Facebook (36.28%) have dominated as data sources across all disciplines throughout 2010-2024, despite being less popular than YouTube among the general public.
Platform usage in research is misaligned with platform popularity among users; YouTube, Reddit, and TikTok are underutilized relative to their public adoption.

The proportion of social media-based papers grew more than threefold from 2010 to the early 2020s but plateaued and declined in 2022-2024, driven primarily by drops in Twitter/X and Facebook usage following API restrictions.
Multi-platform research remains rare: 82.59% of papers use data from a single platform.

Major research topics enabled by social media data—COVID-19, social movements, political communication, misinformation—are matters of substantial public consequence, making data access restrictions consequential for social knowledge.
Researchers are beginning to shift toward alternative platforms (YouTube, TikTok, Instagram) and alternative data collection methods (user-donated data, shared archives) in response to API closures.
methods: Systematic review of 859,105 publications from 59 journals (10 each from Communication, Economics, Political Science, Psychology, Sociology, and interdisciplinary categories; Value in Health excluded) spanning 2010-2024.
Journal selection based on Google Scholar h5-index rankings supplemented by Web of Science searches using social media platform names.

Keyword-based search of journal websites (preferring full-text search) for mentions of eight platforms (Twitter/X, Facebook, LinkedIn, Instagram, Reddit, YouTube, TikTok, Pinterest), yielding 27,951 matched papers; Pinterest later excluded due to poor inter-rater reliability.
Stratified sampling of up to 100 papers per journal for 2010-2023 plus proportional additions for 2024, producing an analytical sample of 5,218 papers.

Two-stage human-in-the-loop annotation: (1) identifying empirical papers; (2) identifying which platforms provided data for empirical analysis. Codebooks developed iteratively with six annotators; inter-rater reliability assessed via Krippendorff's α.

Evaluation of three LLMs (GPT-4.1-mini, GPT-4.1, o4-mini) against human-coded ground truth; o4-mini selected (100% accuracy on empirical identification; ≥91% on platform identification) and applied to all 5,218 papers.
Estimation of total counts in the full matched corpus by scaling sample-based annotations using journal-year-specific sampling ratios.
Topic analysis based on author-supplied keywords from Scopus, constructing a co-occurrence network and applying ensemble Louvain community detection (1,000 runs) to identify topic clusters.
findings: 0.78% of all papers in the corpus (3.15% in social science-only journals) use social media data for empirical research.
Discipline-level prevalence: Communication 12.86%, Political Science 5.11%, Psychology 1.22%, Economics <0.7%, interdisciplinary <0.3%.
Twitter/X used in 50.86% of empirical social media papers; Facebook 36.28%; YouTube 17.11%; Reddit 5.66%; TikTok, Instagram, and LinkedIn smaller shares.
Twitter/X is overrepresented in research relative to its public usage (21% of Americans) while YouTube (84% of Americans) is significantly underrepresented (17.11% of papers).
82.59% of target papers rely on a single platform; only 17.41% use multiple platforms.
The share of target papers tripled (≈235.65% increase in social science disciplines from 2010 to 2024) before plateauing and declining in 2022-2024.

Twitter/X usage shows spikes around 2015-2016 (U.S. presidential election) and 2020-2022 (COVID-19 and 2020 election), driven largely by interdisciplinary journals; declines appear in 2023-2024 following the end of free APIs.
YouTube and especially TikTok show recent growth, with TikTok rising steadily from 2020 onward.

Topic analysis: COVID-19 is the most common topic across disciplines (over 60% of Economics papers using social media data); other prominent topics include social movements, authenticity, political communication, journalism, body image (concentrated in Psychology), ideology, and disinformation.
Communication has the most balanced topic distribution (Gini = 0.38); Economics is most concentrated (Gini = 0.72).
contributions: Provides the first multi-platform, multi-disciplinary systematic baseline of empirical social media research across 15 years and 59 journals, going beyond prior Twitter-centric reviews.
Demonstrates the inadequacy of relying on Web of Science alone (which under-reports relevant papers) and develops an alternative pipeline using journal-website search.
Introduces and validates a reproducible human-in-the-loop LLM annotation methodology for large-scale meta-scientific analysis, with publicly released codebook, prompts, and code.

Documents quantitatively the impact of API closures on academic publishing and identifies emerging shifts toward alternative platforms (TikTok, YouTube) and data collection paradigms (user-donated data, shared archives like ICPSR, NIO).
Maps the topical landscape of empirical social media research, identifying which substantive areas of inquiry are most exposed to platform data access restrictions.
Calls for journal/indexing standardization (e.g., uniform tagging of publication types, mandatory author keywords) to enable future meta-science.
framing: The paper situates itself within ongoing debates about the 'post-API age' of computational social science (Freelon 2018; Freelon et al. 2025), platform governance, and the future of digital trace data research under regulatory regimes such as the EU Digital Services Act. It contributes to meta-scientific scholarship on how data infrastructures shape knowledge production, building on and extending prior, narrower reviews focused primarily on Twitter (DeVito et al. 2017; Karami et al. 2020; Murtfeldt et al. 2025).
